elements.sent-2411-005: A nonequilibrium thermodynamic model is presented for the nonisothermal lithium-ion battery cell.
elements.sent-2411-006: Coupling coefficients, all significant for transport of heat, mass, charge and chemical reaction, were used to model profiles of temperature, concentration and electric potential for each layer of the cell.
elements.sent-2411-007: Electrode surfaces were modelled with excess properties.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extending earlier works, we included lithium diffusion in the electrodes, and explained the cell's thermal signature due to Peltier and Soret effects.
elements.sent-2411-009: We showed that the model is consistent with the second law of thermodynamics, meaning that the entropy production computed at steady state from entropy fluxes is equal to the integral over the sum of flux-force products.
elements.sent-2411-010: The procedure is beneficial in electrochemical cell modelling as it reveals inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-011: The model was solved for typical lithium-ion battery materials.
elements.sent-2411-012: The coupling coefficients for transport of salts and solvents lead to significant concentration polarization.
elements.sent-2411-014: We show that a zero-valued heat flux is not necessarily synonymous with a zero temperature gradient.
elements.sent-2411-015: Results are important for efforts that aim to avoid local hot spots.
elements.sent-2411-016: A program code is made available for testing and applications.
elements.sent-2411-017: The program is designed to solve dynamic boundary value problems posed by the electrode surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-018: Electric energy storage is essential for realization of a greener economy, and lithium-ion batteries are good candidates for such storage.
elements.sent-2411-019: A combined production capacity target of more than \qty{500 {\GWh\per\year of lithium-ion battery cells was announced in Europe alone \cite{Li-ion_battery_cell_production_Europe_2030 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The present work can be seen as a contribution to help realize this target, by enhancing the understanding of battery thermal effects, effects that are known to be detrimental \cite{Sun2015, An2017,Zhang2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Hot spots can arise from internal short circuits and create thermal runaway \cite{Zhang2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The local temperature is also important for battery ageing, for so-called cross-over effects and for electrochemical reactions \cite{Sun2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Thermal effects have been studied in detail in the polymer electrolyte fuel cell, including reversible heat effects, see Cai et al. for a recent review \cite{Cai2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: But such heat sources or sinks have been scarcely investigated in lithium-ion batteries.
elements.sent-2411-025: Spitthoff et al. \cite{spitthoff_peltier_2021 made a first attempt, focusing on initial stages of cell operation and accounting for the coupling that takes place between fluxes of heat and charge.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Recent data from Gullbrekken et al. \cite{gullbrekken_coeff offer an opportunity to also account for the gradients in chemical potential that develop during quasi-stationary cell operation.}
elements.sent-2411-027: To describe coupled transport processes in batteries is a challenge, in particular when it comes to describe the processes taking place at the electrode surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-028: The electrochemical reactivity will vary over the heterogeneous electrode surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-029: Much effort has thus been devoted over the years to find a proper coarse-grained description of all relevant coupled phenomena at the electrode surfaces, see e.g. \cite{Dawlaty2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The derivation of a continuous description of pore-scale events has been central \cite{analysis_polarization_Li_ion, review_param_database_contin_Li_ion, param_physics_based_models_review .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Coupling coefficients were recently shown to be essential for the complex electrolyte in the lithium-ion battery \cite{Fong_Transf_Polyelectrolyte, gullbrekken_coeff .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Nernst-Planck equations, possibly supplied with advection terms, have often been considered to be sufficiently precise for cell modelling, but these equations are now not sufficiently precise; they disregard the coupling between fluxes of heat and mass and charge.
elements.sent-2411-033: Most available models do not deal with coupled transport phenomena at the electrode surfaces as such, see e.g. \cite{Sun2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Rules for coupling of chemical and electrical driving forces in the surfaces differ from those of the bulk phases \cite{net_heterogen .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Current continuous descriptions of charge transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface do not deal with this coupling directly, and can therefore not so easily be used to question common assumptions, for instance that of a constant distribution of reversible heat in the battery.
elements.sent-2411-036: Liu et al. \cite{Liu2024 gives a recent overview of thermally coupled battery models.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The book of Newman \cite{dfn_model_1994 is a good reference for state-of-the-art modelling in electrochemistry.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In order to describe the electrochemical surface with correct coupling or symmetry properties for transports at the electrode surfaces, we have turned to the theory of nonequilibrium thermodynamics (NET) as written for heterogeneous systems \cite{net_heterogen .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Pore-scale events can then be included into a thermodynamic description, by defining the surface as a 2D system of Gibbs surface excess variables.
elements.sent-2411-040: The excess variables are densities of state variables which are integrated over the surface thickness.
elements.sent-2411-041: The surface becomes a 2D autonomous system in the process.
elements.sent-2411-042: The essential theoretical basis was established in the 1970s \cite{bedeaux_bc_net, bedeaux_non_eq_electro .}
elements.sent-2411-043: It was formulated for electrochemical systems much later \cite{net_heterogen , and has even been used to describe shock waves \cite{Maltby2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: This form of NET is particularly suitable when we need to deal with dynamic boundary conditions, i.e. when there are sinks and sources of various types in a surface.
elements.sent-2411-045: This is a trademark of electrochemistry, but also of NET for heterogeneous systems, which makes NET suited for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-046: We shall therefore build on and expand the NET electrochemical cell model of Spitthoff et al. \cite{spitthoff_peltier_2021 in this work.}
elements.sent-2411-047: They calculated the temperature profile across a cell accounting for the coupling of heat and charge, including the initial Peltier heats of the electrode surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, they neglected possible contributions arising from gradients in chemical potential, since they were focusing on the initial stages of cell operation.
elements.sent-2411-049: Battery cells usually operate in quasi stationary state \cite{korthauer_lithium-ion_2018 , however.}
elements.sent-2411-050: With data recently available from Gullbrekken et al. \cite{gullbrekken_coeff there is a possibility to account also for these effects, and improve and extend the work of Spitthoff et al. \cite{spitthoff_peltier_2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: A possibility is also available with NET to test the battery model used for thermodynamic consistency.
elements.sent-2411-052: Common models of electrochemical systems suffice to use mass-, energy- and momentum balances.
elements.sent-2411-053: NET includes also the entropy balance, which can be used for consistency tests.
elements.sent-2411-055: The aim of the work is thus to obtain an improved model for the lithium-ion battery which takes into account thermal phenomena in a thermodynamically consistent manner.
elements.sent-2411-056: We also show that the description in use has a large impact on the outcome of the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-057: The present work concerns stationary states.
elements.sent-2411-058: It is our hope that the general procedure demonstrated can be used in electrochemical cell modelling at large.
elements.sent-2411-059: A robust program is therefore made available for new users.
elements.sent-2411-060: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-061: In Section \ref{sec:cell_layers we present the model for a layered battery cell, and a choice of components for the particular battery in question.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In Section \ref{sec:gov_eq we give the principles of the theory, and the final equations used, while referring to literature for particular derivations.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The numerical solution procedure is outlined in detail in Section \ref{sec:num_sol , with the purpose of helping users of the program provided in GitHub.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Results and discussion follow in Section \ref{sec:results for the base case and variations in that.}
elements.sent-2411-065: All coupling coefficients between heat, mass and charge transport are relevant.
elements.sent-2411-066: This will be pointed out and explained, with emphasis on thermal effects which are new.
elements.sent-2411-067: Particular emphasis will also be made on the possibility to check the model for thermodynamic consistency by using the entropy balance.
elements.section-2411-004: A battery cell of five layers
elements.sent-2411-068: The lithium-ion battery was chosen with the dual purpose to produce new information on the temperature profile of this important battery, but also to show how NET can be used to our advantage in battery modelling, i.e. to obtain a model compatible with the second law of thermodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-069: When NET is used to describe electrochemical cells, the cell is divided into five (or more) layers, see Figure \ref{fig:simplification_surface .}
elements.sent-2411-070: The figure to the right has three bulk layers; of the cathode (grey color), the anode (blue color) and of the electrolyte mixture (yellow color).
elements.sent-2411-071: The layer thicknesses of these three layers can be described on the continuum level using NET (\unit{\um -scale).}
elements.sent-2411-072: These layers can be considered to consist of 3D homogeneous materials with bulk properties.
elements.sent-2411-073: To the left in the figure is shown a close-up of the electrolyte with the adjacent electrode surfaces on much smaller scale.
elements.sent-2411-074: The scale is used to measure changes on the nanometer scale.
elements.sent-2411-075: The electrode surface is defined as the region where the material densities vary from the electrolyte-value to zero.
elements.sent-2411-076: In the construction of Gibbs excess variables, we integrate over these regions.
elements.sent-2411-078: In this manner we obtain coarse-grained variables; or excess properties of the electrode surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-079: The procedure enables us to deal with coupling of fluxes in a 2D-system \cite{net_heterogen , because the scalar components of the fluxes of mass, heat and charge will couple to the scalar chemical reaction.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This is unlike the situation in the bulk phases.
elements.sent-2411-081: Electrode materials are often porous and multiphase.
elements.sent-2411-082: In a 2D-effective description, the properties become average values over the cross-section of the cell.
elements.sent-2411-083: This way of dealing with surfaces is standardly used in NET.
elements.sent-2411-084: It sets the stage for use of dynamic boundary conditions.

elements.sent-2411-086: The electrode reactions take place within this region of molecular thickness.
elements.sent-2411-087: The surfaces extend in space so as to include all positions where the electrochemical reaction can take place.

elements.sent-2411-089: The notation used for 3D- and 2D phases is taken from Ref. \cite{net_heterogen and illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:notation_thermodynamic_system .}}

elements.sent-2411-093: Negative Electrode: Lithium intercalated in graphite, \ce{LiC6.}
elements.sent-2411-096: The electrode surfaces arise between the negative electrode and the electrolyte and between the electrolyte and the positive electrode (orange/dark blue lines in Fig. \ref{fig:simplification_surface ).}
elements.sent-2411-097: The electrolyte in the lithium-ion battery is composed of a lithium salt (\ce{LiPF6 ) and often of two organic co-solvent carbonates, here ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC).}
elements.sent-2411-098: The electrolyte may consist of ternary, even quaternary mixtures.
elements.sent-2411-099: Gullbrekken et al. \cite{gullbrekken_coeff showed that both solvents, EC and DEC, were carried along with the charge transporter in the wall frame of reference, with EC playing a less direct role than DEC.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Therefore they chose to use the EC frame of reference \cite{gullbrekken_coeff .}
elements.sent-2411-101: In a thermodynamic description of the cell, the surface is the natural frame of reference.
elements.sent-2411-102: Gullbrekken et al. \cite{gullbrekken_coeff proposed furthermore to describe the ternary electrolyte as a reacting mixture in equilibrium: \begin{equation}\ce{LiPF6 + 3 DEC <=> \ce{Li+} * 3 DEC + \ce{PF6-}}\end{equation} With the EC frame of reference and this equilibrium condition imposed, there are two independent mass variables in the electrolyte, \ce{LiPF6 and DEC.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: In the presence of thermal, chemical and electrical driving forces, we shall thus find that we need a 4 $\times$ 4 matrix of Onsager coefficients in order to solve the model for the bulk electrolyte.

elements.sent-2411-183: The model was solved with experimental data given in Appendix: Material Properties.
elements.sent-2411-184: A base case was established with the governing equations solved for these data.

elements.sent-2411-187: The present lithium-ion battery cell model involves solution of second-order ordinary differential equations (ODEs), as boundary value problems for each of the bulk phases, see previous section.
elements.sent-2411-188: The equations were implemented and solved in an object-oriented Python program, which is briefly presented in this section.
elements.sent-2411-189: We outline the main elements and provide guidance on how to run a simulation or change material properties.
elements.sent-2411-190: The entire program is available through the GitHub repository at the following link: \url{https://github.com/felixs97/battery_model.git The boundary value problems were conveniently solved in ODEs using a numerical approach (a shooting method).}
elements.sent-2411-191: The method transformed the problem into an initial value problem by adjusting the initial conditions in an iterative way, until the solution satisfied the specified boundary conditions \cite{numerics_engineers .}
elements.sent-2411-192: The scipy Python library was used to address these challenges; in particular solvers like integrate.solve\_ivp.
elements.sent-2411-193: The root-finding function (optimize.fsolve) was utilized to align the solution with the particular boundary conditions of solve\_ivp.
elements.sent-2411-194: By default, the solve\_ivp function uses a Runge-Kutta method of order 5(4) (RK5(4)) \cite{2020SciPy-NMeth .}
elements.sent-2411-195: The optimize.fsolve function provided in the scipy library, is a wrapper around MINIPACK's hybrid algorithm \cite{2020SciPy-NMeth , useful for finding roots of non-linear functions.}
elements.sent-2411-196: A subroutine which calculates the function is provided.
elements.sent-2411-197: This allowed us to compute the Jacobian using a forward-difference approximation \cite{minpack_hybrd .}
elements.sent-2411-198: In our case the function was the difference of the right-hand-side boundary condition and the solution to the set of equations, obtained from an initial guess of the derivative of the temperature.
elements.sent-2411-199: The difference was optimized to be close to zero by changing the initial guess of the derivative of the temperature.
elements.sent-2411-200: The program was divided into four files:.
elements.sent-2411-201: The first two files, (params\_LFP.py and params\_sys.py), contained all material properties and physical constants.
elements.sent-2411-202: This allowed for easy changes of parameter values.
elements.sent-2411-203: The material properties of the base case scenario were those of Spitthoff et al. \cite{spitthoff_peltier_2021 , see Table \ref{table:materials_electrode in the Appendix.}}
elements.sent-2411-204: This provided us with an independent control of their results.
elements.sent-2411-205: The five classes are illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:class_diagram .}
elements.sent-2411-206: The LiionModel class functions as the overarching container, combining all submodels.
elements.sent-2411-207: Users initiate a simulation by creating an instance of this class, triggering the initialization of the associated submodel.
elements.sent-2411-208: Upon initializing, a submodel name is assigned to facilitate access to the corresponding parameter set.
elements.sent-2411-209: A variable dictionary (vars) is initialized to store calculated values.
elements.sent-2411-210: Since each submodel is governed by distinct equations, they can be encapsulated in separate classes, with origin in the Submodel base class.
elements.sent-2411-211: Within each submodel class (e.g., Surface, Electrode, Electrolyte), functions are defined to solve the five sets of governing equations.
elements.sent-2411-212: The entire cell is solved through the solve() method within the LiionModel class.
elements.sent-2411-213: The solving process begins with the determination of the temperature profile across the cell.
elements.sent-2411-214: This is done by solving the set of equations using the shooting method.
elements.sent-2411-215: The left boundary temperature choice and an initial guess for its derivative are applied and the cell is solved from left to right.
elements.sent-2411-216: The resulting temperature on the right-hand side is then compared to the specified right boundary temperature.
elements.sent-2411-217: An optimization process is applied to ensure agreement with the set boundary condition.
elements.sent-2411-218: Once the temperature profile is known, subsequent quantities can be calculated.
elements.sent-2411-219: In order to execute a simulation, we initiated the LiionModel class and provided required parameters.
elements.sent-2411-220: The initialization process included the creation of a numerical mesh for the solution, achieved by invoking the init\_mesh() function and specifying the number of elements for each bulk phase.
elements.sent-2411-221: The surfaces contributed each with a single element.
elements.sent-2411-222: The boundary temperatures were established by calling the boundary\_conditions() function.
elements.sent-2411-223: Subsequently, the model was solved by invoking the solve() function.
elements.sent-2411-224: To visualise and analyse the results, users can utilize the plot() function.
elements.sent-2411-225: The function provides a comprehensive representation of simulation outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-226: By employing these sequential steps, users can effectively run simulations.
elements.sent-2411-227: Discharging of the cell is modelled by setting a positive current density, while charging of the cell is modelled using a negative current density.
elements.sent-2411-228: An example run and output are shown in the Supplementary Information (SI).

elements.subsection-2411-013: The entropy balance: A measure of consistency
elements.sent-2411-331: Within the framework of NET there is an option to assess a model for thermodynamic consistency.
elements.sent-2411-332: Such a check has been used for fuel cells \cite{net_heterogen , but we are not aware of any systematic use.}
elements.sent-2411-333: The validation process entails computing the entropy production in two ways, which must give the same result.
elements.sent-2411-334: The method applies to steady state conditions.
elements.sent-2411-335: At steady state there is no change in entropy density, \(s\), with time, and the entropy balance becomes: \begin{equation}\int_i^o \sigma \ \text{d}x = J^o_s - J^i_s \label{eq:consistency_check}\end{equation} Superscript \(i\) and \(o\) describe here the incoming and outgoing flux of entropy, respectively.

elements.subsection-2411-014: Checking assumptions common in the literature
elements.sent-2411-389: When the entropy balance is everywhere obeyed, we know that a model is thermodynamically consistent.
elements.sent-2411-390: We are then also well set to explore cases outside the base case scenario, and test the model's sensitivity to changes in thermal properties.
elements.sent-2411-391: We have chosen to delve into the impact of changing thermal properties as these are frequently neglected or treated by average procedures.
elements.sent-2411-392: We have therefore explored scenarios, where the total reversible heat effect (the cell entropy change) is distributed uniformly across the cell.
elements.sent-2411-393: This is called the average Reversible Heat scenario (aRH) and is commonly used in literature \cite{thermal_cond_internal_temp_prof, invest_li_ion_batt_echem, DAMAY201537, DU2015327 .}
elements.sent-2411-394: We also studied the case when it is neglected all together, the neglected Reversible Heat scenario (nRH).
elements.sent-2411-395: The effects on temperature profiles and measurable heat fluxes, as explored for the aRH and nRH scenarios, are shown in Fig. \ref{fig:temp_profile_rhe .}
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{temp_profile_reversible_heat.pdf} \caption{Temperature profile of single battery cell \ce{C6}$|$LFP for different assumptions about the reversible heat effects. The thermodynamically consistent model is pictured by a red line. The green line illustrates a model where the total reversible heat effect RHE is distributed uniformly across the cell, while the yellow line shows results where RHE is neglected.} \label{fig:temp_profile_rhe}
elements.sent-2411-396: In the base case scenario, we have used electrode specific Peltier heats derived from measurements \cite{gunnarshaug_reviewreversible_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-397: Notably, we observe heating at the anode surface and cooling at the cathode surface.
elements.sent-2411-398: In the aRH scenario, the total heat effect, $T \Delta S$, is evenly distributed between both surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-399: In the third case, reversible heat effects are neglected altogether, and contributions arise solely from ohmic heating and concentration polarization.
elements.sent-2411-400: Examining the heat fluxes of the cell, shown in Table \ref{table:heat_generation_aRH , we note a significant underestimation of the heat that needs to be dissipated to maintain constant boundary temperatures when we neglect the RH.}
elements.sent-2411-401: Whether we assume local or average aRH does not affect the outcome, because the overall change in \(T \Delta S\) is the same in both cases.
elements.sent-2411-402: However, we observe a marked disparity between local and average RH in the temperature profile.
elements.sent-2411-403: This underscores the importance of the value of the heat flux.
elements.sent-2411-405: It is a rather small effect in the present case which contains one unit cell only.
elements.sent-2411-406: It may grow several degrees above \qty{290 {\K in a cell stack \cite{spitthoff_peltier_2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-407: To incorporate reversible heat effects in a proper manner is thus essential for accurate temperature modelling of electrochemical cells.
elements.sent-2411-408: Specifically, to assume that heat is evenly distributed (aRH) may lead to underestimation of local temperature peaks.

elements.sent-2411-417: The sensitivity to surface material properties is another item of interest.
elements.sent-2411-418: In the absence of measured properties, we used a scaling factor to estimate surface properties.
elements.sent-2411-419: We examined how variations in the scaling factor influenced the temperature profile.
elements.sent-2411-420: The surface thermal conductivities have been estimated from bulk properties using a simple scaling procedure \cite{net_heterogen .}
elements.sent-2411-421: The procedure involves dividing the bulk main thermal conductivity by the product of the surface thickness (\(\delta\)) and a dimensionless scaling coefficient (\(k_i\)).
elements.sent-2411-422: In this manner, the scaling coefficient becomes a factor by which the surface and bulk properties are directly compared.
elements.sent-2411-423: A scaling factor exceeding one represents an excess surface resistance, while a scaling factor below one represents a well conducting interface, not limiting to transport of heat \cite{net_heterogen : \begin{equation}\lambda_s = \frac{\lambda}{\delta k_i} \label{eq:correction_factor}\end{equation} As there are no available measurements for the surface thermal conductivities (Kapitza resistances) that we know of, we shall study the effect of a scaling factor.}
elements.sent-2411-424: Spitthoff et al. \cite{spitthoff_peltier_2021 demonstrated a substantial influence of the chosen scaling factor on both the shape of the temperature profile and the absolute temperatures.}
elements.sent-2411-425: Figure S5 in the SI shows the temperature profile at the surfaces of the cell for various scaling factors, which were varied for the anode and cathode surfaces in the same manner.

elements.sent-2411-433: The internal profiles of temperature, concentration and electric potential in the lithium-ion battery cell has been modelled using the theory of nonequilibrium thermodynamics (NET) as applied to heterogeneous systems \cite{net_heterogen .}
elements.sent-2411-434: We have taken Peltier heats into account, thereby extending an earlier model of the same cell \cite{spitthoff_peltier_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-435: We have shown how the cell model is thermodynamically consistent, providing a method for such checks.
elements.sent-2411-436: The NET theory gives a systematic, general approach to battery modelling.
elements.sent-2411-437: We expect that it can be useful for other electrochemical systems.
elements.sent-2411-438: Coupling effects, the trademark of the theory, are expected to be significant for porous, less well conducting electrodes with gas production or consumption.
elements.sent-2411-439: The results are sensitive to surface transport properties.
elements.sent-2411-440: We have found that assumptions common in the lithium battery literature, to neglect or average out reversible heat effects, can lead to changes in the heat flux in the order of magnitude of \qty{30 {\% of the value obtained from the consistent model.}}
elements.sent-2411-441: The thermodynamic model is essential for accurate battery temperature profile modelling.
